Re5 ignores that and moves your other rook instead, so Black just plays Qxc1+, wins a full rook, and after Kh2, Rg6, Rg5 you're fighting from even further behind. Rd1 gets the hanging rook off that diagonal, and once it's safe you can fight on with c5, Qf3, Qc3, Qd5. When you're already worse, ask first what your opponent's most active piece is attacking — here it was the queen on a3, not the rook you tried to swing to e5.

drills.explanation: c4 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, dxe5 left that piece hanging: after Nxe5 it's simply lost.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs Sadrios (652) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: Right before this you were pressing with a real space edge, and the move that kept it growing was c4 — it hits Black's queen on d5 with tempo while your d4 pawn stays put as a wedge; after Qd7 Nxe4 you're simply a clean pawn up with the queen shoved around. Instead dxe5 releases all that tension for nothing: Black's knight on c6 just recaptures Nxe5, landing a beautifully centralized piece, and after c4 that same knight jumps to c4, attacking your knight on d2 and forcing a trade down to a level game. You swapped a growing advantage for a series of even trades — a move that gains tempo on an enemy piece is usually worth more than releasing central tension.

drills.explanation: Qd2 forks the rook on e1 and the knight on h6 — one of them falls next move. In the game, Qb6 ran into Nxf7+, costing about 3.2 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs Ntk2711 (850) — Caro Kann Defense Karpov Variation
drills.coachNote: Look at f7 for a second — White's knight on h6 and bishop on c4 are both zeroed in on it, and your rook on f8 is the only real guard. Qb6 does nothing about that square, so White just plays Nxf7+, forking your king on h8 and the rook on f8; you're forced to scramble with Kg7, and after Rf3 lifting toward your king you're stuck defending with Qg6 instead of pressing your edge. Qd2 was the move because it attacks first, snapping at c2 and keeping your own initiative alive — after Ng4 Qxc2 you're grabbing material too, so even when White takes on f7 with Bxf7, you answer Bxb3 and you're still comfortably on top instead of scrambling.

drills.explanation: Qh5 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, Qd4 ran into Nxf7+, costing about 1.4 pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs Ntk2711 (850) — Caro Kann Defense Karpov Variation
drills.coachNote: Same knight, same idea two moves earlier — that knight on h6 is still eyeing f7, and your queen on g4 was perfectly placed to harass it. Qd4 retreats away from that job, so White gets Nxf7+ anyway: you have to give back material with Rxf7 Rxf7, and a big chunk of your winning edge disappears instantly. Qh5 was right there instead — it directly attacks the knight on h6, forcing it to hop to f5, and after c4 Ng3 you keep pushing with Qh6 and Bxh7, eating into White's kingside instead of handing material back. When one piece is holding an attack together like that knight, keep your queen pointed at it rather than letting it off the hook.

drills.explanation: a5 was the move — it keeps your pieces coordinated and avoids the tactic that punished your game move. In the game, Ng6 left that piece hanging: after Nxg6 it's simply lost.
drills.title: vs FlokieXIV (796) — Caro Kann Defense Advance Variation
drills.coachNote: Your knight on e7 was fine where it stood, but Ng6 walks it straight into Nxg6 — White's knight on h4 just takes it, and you're forced to recapture fxg6, which rips your kingside into doubled g-pawns and leaves e6 and the dark squares around your king permanently weak. That structural damage is what costs you the 2-plus pawns of eval, and it's why White can later just walk in with a5 and Bc7. The standard plan here is a5, hitting the base of White's queenside space to prepare ...b5 and ...c5, exactly like the engine's Qc7-Qd3 line shows. In the Advance Caro-Kann your minor pieces should stay put while your pawns do the fighting on the queenside.

drills.explanation: Ne5 keeps the attack on the stranded king on f6 alive: the threat is Nd7+, and after Qg8, Nd7+ Kg5 h4+ drags the king even further up the board while your queen sits on h7. Qxb7 won a bishop but allowed Qd5 — a queen trade you can't decline (Qxd5 exd5) — and with queens off, the attack on f6 vanished, about five pawns of advantage.
drills.title: vs sudharsanee (850) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Variation
drills.coachNote: Black's king is stuck out on f6 with nowhere to hide, and that's the whole story here — you don't need the bishop on b7, you need to keep hunting the king. Ne5 keeps that going, and after Qg8 Nd7+ the king is still getting chased while you stay completely winning. Qxb7 grabs a free piece but lets Black's queen come to d5, offering a trade you can't really decline — after Qxd5 exd5 the queens are off, the king is safe again, and you've gone from a crushing +8 attack down to just a solid extra piece. When the enemy king is this exposed, keep the queens on and keep attacking; material can wait.

drills.explanation: Ne5 puts the knight on a square the d4 pawn protects and attacks the knight on g6. If Nxe5, dxe5+ regains the piece with check, and after Qxe5 your Qf3+ keeps queens on against his airy king. Your Qf3+ instead offered the trade Black wanted: Qxf3 gxf3 killed all the pressure, and Nf4 came next, poking at the h3 pawn.
drills.title: vs sudharsanee (850) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Variation
drills.coachNote: Same king on f6 as before — Qf3+ looks forcing because it's check, but it's really just offering a trade: Black plays Qxf3, you recapture gxf3, and the attack you had is gone, replaced by Black's knight jumping into f4 with real activity. Ne5 was the move that kept the pressure on instead — after Nxe5 dxe5+ your pawn recaptures with check, and only once Qxe5 happens does Qf3+ actually land well, chasing the king to g7 with your structure and initiative both intact. Same lesson as last time: don't cash in a winning attack for a queen trade just because a check is available — check whether the trade actually helps you first.

drills.explanation: Both your queen and rook could capture the knight on f4, but Qxf4 is the right way: it wins the knight while the rook on f8 keeps guarding its partner on e8. Rxf4 abandoned that rook — Qxe8+ snapped it off with check, and after Kh7, Qe6 left White completely consolidated. Taking with the wrong piece cost about six pawns.
drills.title: vs prusten63 (873) — Italian Game
drills.coachNote: Both your queen on g5 and your rook on f8 were hitting the knight on f4, but your rooks on e8 and f8 were also defending each other along the back rank. The moment you capture with Rxf4, that protection vanishes, and White has Qxe8+ waiting — the queen glides down the h5-e8 diagonal through g6 and f7, forks your king, and wins a whole rook for free, sending you from roughly equal to losing. Qxf4 was the safe capture: your queen takes the knight instead, the f8 rook stays home guarding e8, and the position stays balanced through Qe2, e3, fxe3, Rxe3. Whenever two of your pieces are covering each other, check what happens to the one left behind before you move the other.

drills.explanation: a6 strikes at White's overextended queenside: after bxa6 Rxa6 your rook enters the game down the a-file, and b5 is met calmly by Ra8. Castling let White play Qe2, hitting your knight on c4 — a piece with no defenders — and you had to scramble with c6 and b5 just to hold it, leaving White a wedge pawn on c6.
drills.title: vs prusten63 (873) — Italian Game
drills.coachNote: You've got a knight sitting nicely on c4, but White's pawns on b4 and b5 are the real story of this position, and a6 was your chance to hit the base of that chain right now — after bxa6 Rxa6 your rook is active on the a-file and the tension resolves on your terms. Castling instead hands White a free tempo: Qe2 develops with threats and the queenside pawns just keep rolling with c6 and b5, and that space is what turns your small edge into a real problem for you. It's not that O-O is unsafe by itself, it's that the pawn break in front of you had a shelf life — once White plays Qe2 and consolidates, undermining b5 gets much harder to find.

drills.explanation: You were a full piece up and just needed to consolidate. c5 gains space, and the knight calmly reroutes to c6 next with b6 and normal development to follow. Ng4 put the knight on a square covered by White's queen all the way back on d1 — Qxg4 took it for nothing, handing the extra piece straight back.
drills.opening: Caro Kann Defense Two Knights Attack
drills.title: vs Feri206 (798) — Caro Kann Defense Two Knights Attack
drills.coachNote: Look at your knight jumping to g4 — nothing of yours defends that square, and White's queen on d1 sits right on the d1-g4 diagonal with a clear runway to it. So White just plays Qxg4 and pockets your knight for nothing; no combination needed, just a hanging piece. You were basically equal before this and now you're down a full piece. The move you want is c5, grabbing queenside space and prepping ...Nc6 and ...Re8 — normal development that doesn't require the opponent's pieces to be looking the other way. Before any knight hop into enemy territory, scan every line the opponent's queen or rooks control first.

drills.explanation: Bxa2 trades off White's light-squared bishop on your terms: after Rxa2 you swing rooks to the e-file with Rae8 and Re6, sitting solidly behind the e5 pawn. Rad8 left the bishops eyeballing each other one move too long — Bh6 came with threats at your king, and after g6, Bxe6 fxe6 left you with doubled, weak e-pawns.
drills.opening: Caro Kann Defense Two Knights Attack
drills.title: vs Feri206 (798) — Caro Kann Defense Two Knights Attack
drills.coachNote: You're completely winning here, up over four pawns, but notice your bishop on e6 and White's bishop on a2 are staring at each other down that long a2-g8 diagonal — that bishop is White's only piece left with any bite. Rad8 ignores it, and White gets to play Bh6, poking at your king and forcing ...g6, then that a2 bishop swoops in with Bxe6, fxe6 and claws back a real chunk of your advantage. The fix is Bxa2 first: trade off White's one dangerous piece right away, Rxa2 Rae8 and you're just cruising with rooks doubled on the e-file. When you're winning big, don't develop past your opponent's only active piece — remove it.

drills.explanation: Nxc6 wins a full rook: the knight captures its counterpart and forks the queen on e7 and the rook on d8, so after Qd6 saves the queen, Nxd8 collects the rook and Qxg5 even adds a pawn. a4 instead gave Black time for Nxe5, trading off your monster knight, and after Qxe5 his other knight landed on e4 with real counterplay.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs TheArtifier (833) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: Your knight on e5 has a huge shot sitting there — Nxc6 forks the queen on e7 and the rook on d8 at once, since a knight landing on c6 hits both squares. Playing a4 ignores that gift, and worse, it lets Black play Nxe5 himself, trading off your monster knight, and after Qxe5 Ne4 the other black knight jumps in hitting your queen and f2, unraveling most of your 4.8 pawn edge. Nxc6 Qd6 Nxd8 Qxd8 and you're just up material, with Qxg5 mopping up another pawn next. Whenever a knight lands somewhere that hits two pieces at once, that's the move to look for first — pawn pushes can wait.

drills.explanation: Nxc6 is a fork: from c6 the knight hits both the queen on e7 and the rook on d8, so bxc6 is forced — wrecking the pawn cover in front of the king on c8. Then Qd3 turns on the knight on e4, and after Nxc5 bxc5 you're clearly on top. b5 abandoned the b4 pawn's guard of your c5 knight, and Nxe5 won material — no recapture worked.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs TheArtifier (833) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: Same idea comes right back — your knight on e5 can still jump into c6, hitting the queen on e7 and rook on d8, and it's still the move to check for before anything else. Instead b5 lets Black grab first with Nxe5, snatching your knight, and after bxa6 bxa6 the black knight lands on b3, forking your rook on a1 and the knight on c5 — that fork is the real cost of leaving e5 unsupported. Nxc6 first keeps the initiative: bxc6, Qd3, and you're still hunting the loose knight on e4. Once a tactic like this appears, check it again on the very next move until you've played it or it's actually gone.

drills.explanation: Rxf7 forces mate in three: your queen on d5 X-rays g8 so f7 can't really be held, and after Rg7+ Kh8, Rg8 is checkmate — the bishop on f6 seals g7 while the queen on d5 guards g8, so the rook can't be taken. Qg5 instead let Black play Rxf2 and keep the counterplay alive.
drills.title: vs yassine3453 (860) — Englund Gambit
drills.coachNote: You're up twenty points here, basically already delivering mate, and the shot is Rxf7 — your rook on e7 grabs the pawn, and because your bishop on f6 covers g7 while your queen on d5 covers g8 along that long diagonal, Rg7+ Kh8 Rg8 is checkmate two moves later. Qg5 throws that away — it forces nothing, and it hands Black's queen on b2 and rook on c2 time to crash through with Rxf2 and Qe5, giving him real counterplay and dropping you from a dead-won mating attack to barely plus-two. When you're this far ahead with pieces swarming the enemy king, your job is to find the forcing mate, not to make a normal-looking move.

drills.explanation: Qxc8 wins the rook on c8 outright — Black never dares recapture, since your bishop on f6 and rook on e7 strangle his king; he tries Qb5, and after Re8 he must throw his queen away with Qf1+ Rxf1. Qd5 let a won game slip: Qxa3, and after Qxd4, Qxh3 stripped your king — nearly fifteen pawns of advantage gone.
drills.title: vs yassine3453 (860) — Englund Gambit
drills.coachNote: Your queen on c4 has a completely free rook sitting on c8 — nothing stops Qxc8, and the game's so lopsided Black can't even afford to recapture, he just has to scramble with Qb5. Instead Qd5 lets Black's queen on b2 go shopping: Qxa3 grabs a pawn, then Qxh3 grabs another, both with tempo because your rooks weren't set up to stop the raid, and your +19.9 landslide shrinks down to about +5. Free material sitting on the board beats a centralizing move every time — when a piece is just hanging like that rook on c8, take it before you do anything else.

drills.explanation: The g5 pawn was attacking your bishop on f4, and Nxg5 meets that head-on — the knight is covered by that same bishop, and after fxg5 Bxg5 Black's kingside pawns are stripped while his pieces retreat to passive squares like Nf6 and Nb8 as Qf3 builds pressure. Bf5+ dropped a whole piece instead: e6 blocked the check and hit the bishop, and after Bc2, gxf4 won your dark-squared bishop.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs jatin221272 (817) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: Your bishop on f4 has been hanging to Black's pawn on g5 this whole time — g5 attacks f4 directly — and instead of dealing with that you're sending your OTHER bishop to f5 for a check that accomplishes nothing. Black just plays e6, kicks it straight back, and then calmly takes gxf4, and you've lost a full piece for a spite check. Nxg5 is what actually solves the problem — your knight grabs the pawn that was attacking Bf4 in the first place, and after fxg5 Bxg5 you're not down anything, with Black's king still stuck on d7 and unable to castle. Before giving a check, ask what it actually threatens — if the answer is nothing, look at what's hanging instead.

drills.explanation: Nxg5 was the shot: after fxg5 Bxg5 you get two pawns for the knight and Black's king on d7 is stranded, with Qf3 coming to hit the loose d5 pawn (the simple e4 was strong too). Bf5+ backfired — e6 blocked with tempo, the bishop crawled back to c2, and gxf4 scooped up your dark-squared bishop, which the g5 pawn had been eyeing all along.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs jatin221272 (817) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: Two moves earlier, same story: your bishop on f4 sits attacked by the pawn on g5, and Bf5+ is just a check that buys nothing — Black answers e6, your bishop has to run, and gxf4 collects the piece for free right after. With Black's king already stuck on d7, unable to castle, the real move is e4, ripping the center open — yes Black still grabs gxf4, but exd5 next hits the knight on c6, and Qa4+ comes with check, chasing the king around while you're way ahead in development. Even conceding the bishop is a real investment in attacking a king that can't get to safety — Bf5+ was just noise.

drills.explanation: Your knight on e4 was attacked by the d3 pawn, and Nd6 is the safe retreat — White wins the e5 pawn with Rxe5+, but Be7 blocks, Bg6 follows, and you're only down a pawn with pieces coming out. Ng5 ran the knight straight onto the c1-bishop's diagonal: Bxg5 won it outright, and White grabbed e5 with the knight as well.
drills.title: vs Stwilso1 (836) — Ruy Lopez Opening Berlin Defense
drills.coachNote: Your knight's sitting on e4, and when you jump it to g5 you're walking it straight into the c1 bishop's diagonal — c1 to g5 runs clean through d2-e3-f4 with nothing of yours covering it. White just plays Bxg5 and the knight's gone for free; then f6 kicks the bishop, Nxe5 grabs your e5 pawn too, and your king's still stuck in the center while you're down real material. Nd6 was the move instead — it tucks the knight somewhere safe and keeps developing, and even though White snags the e5 pawn with Rxe5+ Be7, you're still playing a normal game. Before you drop a piece on a square, check what's already aiming at it.

drills.explanation: Your knight on g5 was attacked by the bishop on c1, defended only by your queen along the d8–g5 diagonal — and Be7 blocked that exact diagonal. Bxg5 won the knight, and after Be6, Bxe7 Qxe7 left White a clean piece up. Ne6 just steps to safety; after Qf3 Qf6 Nxc6 Bxd3 the position stays a fair fight.
drills.title: vs Stwilso1 (836) — Ruy Lopez Opening Berlin Defense
drills.coachNote: Same problem, one move later: that knight's still parked on g5, still hanging to the bishop on c1, and Be7 develops a completely different piece instead of rescuing it. White just plays Bxg5 for free, and since your new bishop on e7 isn't well defended either, Bxe7 Qxe7 follows right after — you've lost real material for almost nothing. Ne6 was the fix: it walks the knight off g5 to a square where it's simply not attacked, and after Qf3 Qf6 Nxc6 the position stays level. When one of your pieces is hanging, that's the emergency to solve before you develop anything else.

drills.explanation: Bc7 hits the queen on d8 and grabs a square deep in Black's camp: after Qd7, b6 locks the bishop on c7 permanently, cramping the whole queenside while Ra4 swings the rook toward the action. Your e4 gave Black a free move for Bd7, developing with a hit on the b5 pawn — the same Bc7 idea then came a tempo late, and the queen slipped away to c8.
drills.title: vs slammerman001 (850) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: You've got a small edge here, and there's a sharper move than e4 sitting on the board: Bc7 drops your bishop from f4 onto c7, aiming straight down at the queen on d8, so Black has to spend a move dealing with it — Qd7, and then b6, Nc6, Ra4 all follow with you dictating play. e4 just pushes a pawn instead, and that hands Black the tempo he needed to play Bd7, tucking the bishop back so your c7 idea isn't nearly as sharp anymore since the queen now has c8 to run to. When you spot a move that comes with a threat, play that before the quiet move that just improves your position.

drills.explanation: Qc5 does three jobs: it confronts the queen on b6, attacks the knight on e7, and keeps the b5 pawn defended. After Qd8, Bd6 piles onto e7, and Bxe7 Rxe7 leaves you in full control of the dark squares. Qc3 left b5 with no defender at all — Bd7 came immediately, and after your too-late Qc5, Bxb5 took the pawn for free.
drills.title: vs slammerman001 (850) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: One move later the same idea shows up again, only stronger: Qc5 leans on the queen on b6 and sets up Bd6 next, which hits the rook on f8 — if Black dodges with Qd8, you get Bd6, Re8, Bxe7, winning the knight on e7 clean. Qc3 skips that move order and just centralizes, so Black gets a free move to play Bd7, and by the time your queen does swing to c5 the position's changed — Bxb5 is right there, and your b5 pawn falls because nothing's watching it anymore. Same idea, wrong order: when you see a two-move plan like Bd6 hitting e7, play the move that keeps the pressure on right now.

drills.explanation: Nf5+ is a fork: it checks the king on g3 and attacks the rook on g7 at the same time. After Kf4 you play Nxg7, winning the rook and flipping the game — roughly twelve pawns of swing. Nb7 instead walked into Rc7+, and after Kd8 the rook simply captured the knight on b7.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs OdisseoAP (826) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: You're crushing this position, up more than four pawns, and there's a check that just ends it: Nf5+ forks the king on g3 and the rook on g7 at once — the king has to move, say Kf4, and then Nxg7 simply pockets the rook. Instead Nb7 sends the knight somewhere it does nothing and, worse, leaves it trapped: White plays Rc7+, your king's forced to d8, and Rxb7 eats the knight for free. That's not a small slip — a completely winning position turns into one where you're down a piece with nothing to show for it. When you're up big, scan first for checks and forks that finish the job.

drills.explanation: Bxg3+ wins a pawn with check, and after the forced Kxg3, Rxd1 scoops the rook — d1 had no defender at all, so you net rook and pawn for the bishop. Rd7 put your rook on a square nothing defends (the knight on c6 doesn't cover d7): Rxd7 took it for free, and after Bd6, bxc6 grabbed your knight as well.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs OdisseoAP (826) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: You're up more than six pawns here, completely winning, and Bxg3+ cashes it in immediately — your bishop grabs the pawn on g3 with check, the king's forced to recapture there, and then Rxd1 scoops up White's rook for free since nothing guards it, leaving you up a full rook. Instead Rd7 drops your rook onto a file White's own rook already occupies — same d-file — so Rxd7 just wins it outright, and your six-pawn cushion turns into a loss on the spot. That's the whole swing: check who else is sitting on the file or diagonal before you land a piece there, especially your rook.

drills.explanation: Qxb7+ forces checkmate: the queen grabs b7 and checks the king on e7 along the wide-open seventh rank, Black must block with Qc7, and Qxc7 is mate — your knight on f6 covers the escape squares e8 and d7, and the e5 pawn guards the knight. exd4 was a pawn grab with mate on the board; after Bg7 Black started breathing again.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs pisitto999 (814) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: You're already completely winning, and there's mate on the board: Qxb7+ takes the pawn on b7 with check straight down the 7th rank into the king on e7 — Black's only reply is Qc7 to block, and then Qxc7 is checkmate, the king has nowhere left to go. exd4 is a perfectly reasonable-looking recapture, but it throws the mate away and lets Black scramble back with Bg7 and dxc5 instead of the game just ending. You still won in the end, but that's the difference between finishing in two moves and grinding it out. When the enemy king's this exposed, scan for checks before you reach for the obvious recapture.

drills.explanation: Rd7+ is a deflection: the queen on c7 has to capture, and Nxd7 then forks the rook on b8 and the bishop on f8 — you've won queen for rook, with Rd1 coming to support the knight. Nd7 tried the fork without the preparation: Bg7 simply stepped the bishop out, nothing was won, and Rd6 got met by Rbc8. The open d-file was the key — use it with check first.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs pisitto999 (814) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: There's another one of these hiding here: Rd7+ sacrifices your rook with check, Black's king on e7 forces Qxd7 as the only way to answer it, and then Nxd7 scoops up the queen with your knight — you've traded a rook for a full queen. Nd7 instead just retreats the knight quietly, and after Bg7, Rd6 you're still doing well but that winning shot is gone, which is why the eval drops close to four pawns. Notice the pattern: when your rook can check on a square only the enemy queen is able to cover, look at what happens after they're forced to take it — your knight was right there waiting to profit.

drills.explanation: Your queen on a4 was attacked by the knight on c5 and had to reach safety first — Qa3 does it. The Qh4 attacking idea was right but needed f6 first: after f6 gxf6, Black's own pawn on f6 blocks the e7–h4 diagonal, and only then is Qh4 strong. Played immediately, Qh4 landed right on the e7-queen's diagonal — Qxh4 won your queen on the spot.
drills.title: vs Iamastalemate (860) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: See that diagonal running from e7 to h4? f6 and g5 are completely empty, so the instant your queen lands on h4 it's sitting on the same line as black's queen on e7 — Qxh4 just wins it outright, no recapture, nothing. You were already fighting uphill at -4.5, but this turns a tough defense into a lost game on the spot. Before you place your queen anywhere, trace every diagonal and file through that square and ask what's already sitting on it. Qa3 sidesteps that diagonal completely and lets you keep grinding with c4 and g4 instead of handing over the game.

drills.explanation: Your e5 pawn was attacked by the d6 pawn; exd6 resolves it cleanly, and after cxd6 you develop normally with Nc3 and Nf3, dead level. Pushing e6 instead placed the pawn on a square Black's f7 pawn covers: fxe6 ate it for free, and after Bd3 Nf6 c4 you were simply a pawn down out of the opening.
drills.title: vs Iamastalemate (860) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: Your pawn on e5 only survives if nothing can take it — pushing it to e6 puts it directly in front of black's f7 pawn, which just grabs it with fxe6. You had a real edge here at +2.5, built on that advanced pawn cramping black's position, and pushing it one square further hands that edge right back for nothing. Instead of marching past the target, exd6 removes the tension by capturing first: after cxd6 you develop cleanly with Nc3 and Nf3, keeping the structure and the advantage intact. When a pawn of yours is this far forward, check exactly what recaptures it before you push it again.

drills.explanation: Your queen on e5 was attacked by the knight on f3, and Qe6 was the safe square — it stays active and out of reach, and if Nd4 chases it again, Qe5 returns with White having nothing better than repeating. Qd6 stepped onto the open d-file instead: Rad1 hit the queen with your own rook on d8 lined up behind it, and after Qb4, Rxd8+ Bxd8 handed White the file and the initiative.
drills.title: vs Iamastalemate (859) — Polish Opening
drills.coachNote: Notice your queen and rook end up on the same file here — Qd6 lines your queen up right in front of your own rook on d8, and that's exactly the file White's other rook wants to join with Rad1. Once that rook arrives, your queen gets skewered in front of the rook and has to jump away with Qb4, and Rxd8+ picks off the exchange with Bxd8 forced back — a level game at +0.2 turns genuinely unpleasant. Qe6 avoids stacking your queen and rook on one line in the first place, meets Nd4 with Qe5, and after Nf3 you're heading toward a safe repetition instead of a forced concession.

drills.explanation: Bxb4 just takes a free pawn — the whole point of testing the Polish. White regains it with Bxe5, but after Nf6, c3, Be7 you've swapped his wing pawn for your e-pawn and developed smoothly. Nc6 guarded e5 but walked into b5: the knight had to retreat to e7, and Bxe5 took the pawn anyway — White kept his extra pawn and gained time.
drills.title: vs Iamastalemate (859) — Polish Opening
drills.coachNote: Take a beat and look at White's pawn on b4 — the bishop just moved to b2, but that bishop doesn't defend b4 at all, so your bishop on f8 can simply grab it for free with Bxb4. Instead, Nc6 develops naturally but ignores that free pawn, and it backfires: White plays b5, kicking your knight, and once it retreats to e7 it no longer guards e5, so Bxe5 grabs a second pawn. Bxb4 wins the pawn cleanly, and after Bxe5 Nf6 you're still comfortable with a full extra pawn. Always scan for hanging pawns before you spend a move developing past them.

drills.explanation: Nd4 does everything at once: it saves the knight from the d5 pawn and attacks White's queen on f3, winning a free tempo. After Qd1 you follow with Bf5 and b5, developing with real energy. Nce7 retreated to a passive square that blocks your own f8 bishop, and gave White time for d4, building a big centre unchallenged.
drills.title: vs Aldr_in (958) — Kings Pawn Opening
drills.coachNote: Your knight on c6 has a far better square available — Nd4 jumps into the center and directly attacks White's queen sitting on f3, since d4 to f3 is one knight hop away. Instead, Nce7 retreats passively, blocks your own bishop on f8 and queen, and lets White play d4 for free, grabbing the center and leaving you worse off by a couple pawns' worth of position. Nd4 wins a tempo by forcing the queen to move — after Qd1 you develop with Bf5 and b5, gaining space while White's queen is still getting organized. When a knight move both centralizes and hits a piece, that beats a quiet retreat almost every time.

drills.explanation: Kb6 steps to safety, and play stays balanced: Rh5 Rg8, then Rxf5 for him and Rxg3 for you — an even pawn-grab. Kd6 walked onto exactly the square the knight on e5 forks from f7: Nf7+ hits king and rook together, and Ke7 Nxd8 Kxd8 costs you a rook for a knight. From e5, f7 was the springboard covering both d6 and d8.
drills.title: vs Aldr_in (958) — Kings Pawn Opening
drills.coachNote: Your king on d6 is one knight-hop away from trouble — Nf7+ forks you the moment you step there, and after Ke7 the real point shows up: Nxd8+ isn't just a fork, it's a discovered check from White's rook on h7, which was staring down the 7th rank the whole time with the knight blocking it. That costs you the rook on d8 outright, turning a bad -4.0 into something close to lost. Kb6 heads the other way, toward your own b5 pawn where no knight check is waiting, and after Rh5 Rg8 you're actually trading rook activity and grabbing the g3 pawn back. In knight endings, check every square your king could land on for a fork before you move it.

drills.explanation: The e5 pawn was attacking your bishop on f4, and Nxe5 turns the threat around: it wins the pawn, and after Nxe5 Bxe5 your bishop sits beautifully in the center eyeing g7, leaving Black scrambling with Qg6 before you castle and meet Bf5 calmly. Castling first simply dropped the bishop — exf4 took it for free, and you were a piece down by move nine.
drills.title: vs dangalindo97 (884) — London System
drills.coachNote: Your bishop on f4 is hanging right now — black's pawn on e5 can just capture it with exf4, and castling does nothing to address that, so O-O lets that pawn win a whole piece for free. Instead, Nxe5 solves the problem by removing the attacker first: your knight takes the pawn, black's knight on c6 recaptures on e5, and your bishop on f4 swoops in with Bxe5 since nothing's left to defend that square — you come out a clean pawn ahead instead of a bishop down. That's the habit to build: whenever one of your pieces is under direct attack, deal with it before you spend a move on something unrelated like castling.

drills.explanation: Qg4 stepped straight onto the c8-bishop's open diagonal — c8 to g4 had nothing in the way, and Bxg4 took the queen; after h3, Be6 the bishop went home and Black was simply up material. Qh5 was the safe square, or better yet Nd2 first: develop the stranded b1 knight, tuck the queen on e1, and Nf3 finally challenges the bishop on g5.
drills.title: vs dangalindo97 (884) — London System
drills.coachNote: Take a look at the diagonal from c8 to g4 — d7, e6, and f5 are all completely empty, so black's bishop on c8 has a clear shot straight down to wherever your queen lands on g4. Qg4 walks right onto that diagonal and Bxg4 just wins your queen for a bishop, turning an already-rough -4.0 into a near-certain loss. Before putting your queen on any square, scan the diagonals reaching it, especially from pieces that haven't moved yet, like that bishop on c8. Qh5 avoids that diagonal entirely, and though you're still worse, lines like Be7, Nd2, Rxf4 keep you in a fighting, playable position instead of just losing the queen.

drills.explanation: Both Black bishops were loose — f5 to your bishop on d3, g5 to your knight — but capture order decided everything. Bxf5 takes the one with a counter-capture available, since the f5 bishop and your d3 bishop attack each other; after gxf5, Nd4 centralizes powerfully. Nxg5 allowed the in-between move Bxd3, and after Qxd3 Qxg5 Black had won the piece right back.
drills.title: vs LeoAlayon (759) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: You've got two minor pieces that can get hurt here — your bishop on d3 and the knight you're about to send to g5 — because Black's bishop on f5 is aimed straight at d3 the whole time. Nxg5 grabs a piece but ignores that: Black answers Bxd3, and after Qxd3 Qxg5 it's an even trade of your bishop-and-knight for two bishops, except your queen then gets chased by f4 and you've quietly lost the bishop pair and about two pawns of position. Bxf5 first removes the piece attacking d3 before any of that starts; after gxf5 Nd4 Nxd4 cxd4 h4 you're the one hitting the surviving g5 bishop with tempo. Capture order matters — deal with the threat before you cash in.

drills.explanation: Bxf5 resolves the bishop standoff your way: after Bxg5 Nxg5 Qxg5 you have Bd7 — the bishop lands in Black's camp hitting the knight on c6 and the rook on e8. dxe5 handed Black the move order: Bxg5 came first, and after Bxf5 gxf5 Nd4 the trades fizzled — about a pawn and a half lost purely to capturing in the wrong sequence.
drills.title: vs LeoAlayon (759) — Indian Game
drills.coachNote: Same pattern one move earlier: your bishop on g5 and your bishop on d3 are both potential targets, since Black's bishop on f5 is aimed right at d3, and dxe5 just grabs a center pawn instead of dealing with it. Black answers Bxg5 for free, and after Bxf5 gxf5 Nd4 you're down a piece with Black's knight landing on the strong d4 outpost — a pawn snack that cost you two pawns of evaluation. Bxf5 first removes the piece eyeing d3; after Bxg5 Nxg5 Qxg5 Bd7 Reb8 the trades happen on your terms and your last bishop lands actively on d7. When two of your pieces are simultaneously loose, fix that before you go pawn-hunting.

drills.explanation: Your queen on a8 had the bishop on c8 pinned against the queen on d8, and Rfc1 — either rook works, but the f1 rook keeps a1 at home — piles onto it down the fully open c-file. Black's best is to bail out with Be6, when Qxd8+ Nxd8 trades into a pleasant endgame for you. g3 let the moment slip: d5 and then Nd6 covered c8, and the target wriggled free.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs hansk74 (917) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: You're completely winning here — queen deep on a8, way up in the eval — but g3 spends a move on king comfort while your queen is actually the one who needs looking after. Black answers d5, and with Rfc1 Nd6 the knight starts cutting off the a8 queen's escape squares, forcing an awkward Kh1 just to survive a problem you let creep in, and two pawns of your advantage evaporate. Rfc1 does the job instead: it develops with a threat against the bishop on c8, and after Be6 Qxd8+ Nxd8 d5 Bxd5 you trade queens straight into a completely winning endgame instead of babysitting a stranded queen. When you're up big, the question every move should be 'does this keep my initiative going,' not 'am I safe.'

drills.explanation: Nxe4 wins the e4 pawn cleanly — d5 can't recapture, because that would open the b3–g8 diagonal from your queen straight onto Black's king. After f5 the knight slides back to g3, and c4 starts prying at d5 next. Nh2 retreated for nothing: f5 cemented Black's pawn on e4, and you had to play f3 to challenge it anyway, a tempo and a pawn poorer.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.title: vs hansk74 (917) — Queens Pawn Opening Accelerated London System
drills.coachNote: Check the pawn on e4 before you retreat your knight — nothing defends it. Nh2 tucks the knight away, but it skips the free pawn sitting right there, and Black gets to play f5, locking that pawn in as a permanent wedge that cramps your position for the rest of the game. Nxe4 simply takes it: after f5 Ng3 g5 c4 Be6, you're up a clean pawn with your knight rerouted to a fine square on g3, and it's Black's kingside pawns getting loose, not yours. Build the habit: when an enemy pawn is sitting in the center with nothing guarding it, that's not a detail to notice later — it's the move to play now.

drills.explanation: White's queen on e2 stares down the e-file at your king on e8 — Kf8 steps out of that pin, and after White castles, Re8 takes the open file with b5 coming next. Be7 planted the bishop directly into the pin: White castled long, and the moment you castled kingside the bishop on e7 lost its defender — Qxe7 won it clean.
drills.title: vs timis511 (783) — Scotch Game
drills.coachNote: Your bishop only gets to sit safely on e7 because your king on e8 is still guarding that square — the moment you follow up with O-O next move, the king leaves e8, e7 is undefended, and White's queen on e2 is staring straight down the open e-file at it. That's exactly what happens: O-O-O, O-O, and then Qxe7 just wins your bishop for nothing, a full piece gone that turns your small -0.7 edge into a loss. Kf8 instead sidesteps the whole problem — the king relocates without hanging anything, and after O-O Re8 Ne4 b5 Bd5 you're contesting the e-file yourself and developing normally. Before you commit a piece to a square, ask what's still defending it once your next move happens, not just right now.

drills.explanation: Nd7 saves the attacked knight and, crucially, plugs the a4–e8 diagonal to your king; after exd6 you castle long, White's Qd5 gets kicked by Nb6, and Qf5+ is only a check. dxe5 ripped open the d-file with your king still on e8: Bb5+! and the cheapest escape was Qxb5 Nxb5 — queen for bishop. The capture looked free; the open lines behind it weren't.
drills.title: vs timis511 (783) — Scotch Game
drills.coachNote: You're up roughly a piece here, but your king is still on e8, and dxe5 grabs a pawn without noticing your queen on a5 is the only piece that can block a check on b5 — no knight on c6, nothing to interpose. White plays Bb5+, you're forced into Qxb5, and Nxb5 forks your queen off the board for a mere bishop, a huge swing that turns a winning game into a fight. Nd7 keeps things calm and gets you ready to castle queenside; after exd6 O-O-O your king tucks safely on c8 before that check ever exists, and Nb6 later hits the bishop on c4 with tempo. King stuck in the center means: get safe before you grab pawns.

drills.explanation: Bxc3+ wins a clean piece: the bishop takes the knight with check, the king steps back to c1, and Qd4 then dominates — after Rd1, Qc5 keeps the extra material with a raging attack. Qxb3+ gave away the queen for far too little: the c2 pawn simply captured it, and even after Rxb3+ Kc2 Rxc3+ you had only a knight and two pawns for her. The knight on c3 was the loose piece; the b3 pawn was defended.
drills.opening: Ruy Lopez Opening Old Steinitz Defense
drills.title: vs iwanpagun (934) — Ruy Lopez Opening Old Steinitz Defense
drills.coachNote: Before you play any capture with check, ask what's still guarding that square — the pawn on c2 defends b3, so Qxb3+ just hands White your queen for nothing; cxb3 and it's simply gone, check or no check. You claw back some material with Rxb3+ Kc2 Rxc3+, but you've traded your queen for a rook and a couple of pawns, which is why a completely winning position at -2.0 flips into a loss. Bxc3+ was the check to give instead — it trades your bishop for the knight on c3 evenly, and after Kc1 Qd4 Rd1 Qc5 b4 your queen floods in from d4 and c5 with White's king still exposed, no material given up. Check only helps if the piece giving it survives — look at what recaptures first.

drills.explanation: The bishop on g5 was hanging to your h6 pawn — hxg5 takes it, and White gets nothing back: after g4, Nh7 keeps the extra piece, and Nd5 is answered by Bxd5 Rxd5. Qb7 gave White the tempo to bail out with Bxf6, and once your e7 bishop recaptured on f6, the d6 pawn lost its guard — Rxd6 followed at once.
drills.opening: Ruy Lopez Opening Old Steinitz Defense
drills.title: vs iwanpagun (934) — Ruy Lopez Opening Old Steinitz Defense
drills.coachNote: Your pawn on h6 can just eat the bishop on g5 — nothing defends it, it's a free piece sitting there. Qb7 shuffles the queen instead and ignores it completely, so White plays Bxf6 trading off your knight, and once Bxf6 Rxd6 happens, your d6 pawn is gone too — over four pawns of evaluation lost for a queen move that did nothing. hxg5 just takes the free piece; after g4 Nh7 Nd5 Bxd5 Rxd5 you're simply up a clean bishop for the rest of the game while White scrambles for activity. Before making a quiet developing move, scan the board for anything of yours that's hanging — and anything of theirs, too.

drills.explanation: hxg5 wins a pawn and creates a passed pawn of your own: after Rd8, Rh1 drops your rook to the back rank where it holds up Black's e-pawn — e2 is met calmly — and gxh6 sets your runner loose. Kc2 was too passive: Rg8 and Kd4 activated Black's rook and king together, his connected threats growing while your rook sat tied to h5.
drills.title: vs eddyfrias (879) — Caro Kann Defense
drills.coachNote: Right here hxg5 was sitting there for the taking — your h4 pawn scoops the g5 pawn, clears the kingside, and hands you your own connected passers, so after ...Rd8 Rh1 e2 gxh6 you're racing your g/h-pawns against Black's e-pawn with real chances. Kc2 shuffles the king and does nothing about that pawn, so Black plays Rg8 first, permanently guarding g5, and then marches the king to d4 to babysit the queening e-pawn while your pawns just sit there doing nothing. When a capture is only available for one move, take it before your opponent gets the chance to defend it.

drills.explanation: hxg5 first — dissolve Black's kingside pawns while your king alone handles the passer: e2 is met by Kd2, then Re8, Ke1 and the e-pawn is stopped dead. Kd1 allowed g4 instead: the g-pawn became a passed runner with the rook coming to g8 behind it, while your own rook drifted from f5 back to f1, purely defensive.
drills.title: vs eddyfrias (879) — Caro Kann Defense
drills.coachNote: Same story one move later — hxg5 is still there, and it's more urgent now since Black's e-pawn is a step closer to queening. After hxg5 e2 Kd2 Re8 Ke1, your king gets right in front of the pawn on e1 and you're still fighting. Kd1 instead lets Black push g4, so now it's not just the e3 pawn — Black has a second passer rolling, and your rook on h5 ends up chasing shadows while Black's king and rook organize the win. Twice in a row you looked at the king instead of the loose pawn your rook was already attacking.

drills.explanation: White's bishop on e4 was attacking your rook on a8, and Bb5 answers with a counter-threat against the rook on f1 — so after Bxa8, Bxf1 and the damage cancels out. Qxc5 grabbed a pawn but ignored the corner: Bxa8 took the rook, and White's bishop slid back along the long diagonal to f3 in time to cover f1.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs LeJuAl (890) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: Look at the diagonal from White's bishop on e4 down to a8 — b7, c6, d5 are all empty, so your rook on a8 is hanging the moment you don't deal with it. Qxc5 grabs a pawn but ignores that whole diagonal, and White just plays Bxa8, winning the exchange outright; the follow-up ...Bb5 Bf3 Bxf1 gets some material back but it's still a net loss. Bb5 was the move because it puts your own bishop on that same diagonal, so if White grabs Bxa8 you answer Bxf1 immediately — now it's rook-for-bishop on both sides, even instead of one-sided. Before grabbing a pawn, check what open lines just got aimed at your own back rank.

drills.explanation: bxc5 wins a clean pawn: the d4 pawn daren't recapture because it's the lone defender of the knight on e5, so White retreats with Qc2 and you keep the extra pawn. Nxe5 released the tension backwards — dxe5 hit your f6 knight, and after Ne4 Nxe4 dxe4 everything traded off with White's structure intact and the c5 pawn still cramping your queenside.
drills.opening: Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.title: vs LeJuAl (890) — Queens Pawn Opening Zukertort Chigorin Variation
drills.coachNote: The pawn on c5 is just sitting there undefended — no b4 pawn behind it, nothing covering it — so bxc5 wins it clean and keeps everything else in place. Nxe5 trades knights instead, but after dxe5 White's pawn lands on e5 attacking your knight on f6, forcing Ne4 Nxe4 dxe4, and you never actually collect that free pawn on c5. You still won this game, but the point stands: when a pawn is hanging for free, take it before starting a trade that just burns your chance to win it. Scan for undefended pawns before reaching for a piece trade.

drills.explanation: After White grabbed your e5 pawn, the queen gets it straight back — Qd4 forks the knight on e5 and the pawn on e4, so Nf3 saves the knight but Qxe4+ regains the pawn with check, and the queen trade leaves a level endgame (Qe7, hitting the knight the same way, also works). Qf6 attacked from the wrong angle: d4 simply propped up the knight, and you stayed a pawn down.
drills.opening: Ruy Lopez Opening Morphy Defense Exchange Variation
drills.title: vs justme_abhi (895) — Ruy Lopez Opening Morphy Defense Exchange Variation
drills.coachNote: Qe7 was the move because it puts your queen on the e-file against both the knight on e5 and the pawn on e4 sitting behind it — if the knight ever moves, Qxe4+ just wins the pawn with check and forces a trade into an endgame where you're simply up material with White's king stuck in the middle. Qf6 looks similar but sits one file over, so it puts zero pressure on e4, and White calmly plays d4 to prop up the knight, then Nc4 hits your bishop on d6 and kicks your queen around with tempo. When two squares look similar for your queen, pick the one that adds a second attacker to an existing target.

drills.explanation: Your queen on f6 was attacked by the knight on g4 — Qh4 saves her with interest: the knight is hit twice (queen plus the c8 bishop) and defended only by the queen on d1, so it retreats to e3, and Qxe4 collects the central pawn for free. Bxg4 saved the queen by donating your bishop — Qxg4 recaptured, and after h5, Qf3 White stood fine.
drills.opening: Ruy Lopez Opening Morphy Defense Exchange Variation
drills.title: vs justme_abhi (895) — Ruy Lopez Opening Morphy Defense Exchange Variation
drills.coachNote: Qh4 was right there for your queen, attacking the knight on g4 along the open 4th rank with nothing defending it — that wins the piece clean, and after Ne3 Qxe4 you grab the e4 pawn too. Bxg4 goes after the same knight with the wrong piece: White just recaptures Qxg4, so it's only an even bishop-for-knight swap, and then h5-Qf3 chases your queen around and hands White the tempo you should've kept. The difference between winning a piece and trading it evenly comes down to which of your pieces can actually capture it for free — check that before deciding who takes.

drills.explanation: Bxc3 wins the knight and — crucially — keeps your queen on d6 plugging the d-file, so Qa8+ can be met by Kd7, and even after Rxd6+ Kxd6 Qxb7 you're still fighting. Qb6 attacked White's queen but stepped off d6, handing the d-file to the rook on d1 — d7 was no longer an escape square, and Qa8 was checkmate on the spot.
drills.title: vs paam66 (1011) — Grob Opening
drills.coachNote: Your queen on d6 had one critical job: blocking White's rook on d1 from ever reaching d7, the only flight square your king on c8 has since b7 and c7 are jammed with your own pawns. The instant Qb6 steps off d6, that file opens all the way to your king, and Qa8# is simply mate — check along the 8th rank with nowhere left to go. Bxc3 was sitting right there instead, grabbing the knight while your queen stayed put guarding d7; even the sharper Qa8+ Kd7 Rxd6+ line leaves you ahead on material. Before moving a piece that's shielding your king, always ask what square it's actually covering.

drills.explanation: Qh4 swings the queen out with Bf4 to follow, attacking White's queen — in the line he must bail with Qxd4, and after Rxd4 Rxd4 you've given rook and knight for the queen, the stronger army here. Nf5 abandoned d4 and unblocked the e3–a7 diagonal: Qa7! dove in beside your king, and after c6, Nc5 piled on, forcing Bb8 just to evict the queen.
drills.title: vs paam66 (1011) — Grob Opening
drills.coachNote: You were completely winning here, and Qh4 keeps it that way — it hits the knight on e4 along the open 4th rank, and after Kb1 Bf4 White is forced into Qxd4, letting your rook take it right back with Rxd4, netting a full queen for a knight. Nf5 doesn't threaten anything concrete, so White gets a free tempo to play Qa7, infiltrating near your king, then Nc5 jumps in with real threats against b7 and e6, and your huge edge shrinks fast. When you're up material and attacking, favor the move that keeps hitting a piece over one that just repositions — forcing moves keep the initiative in your hands.

drills.explanation: Rdc1 doubles rooks on the open c-file with the brutal threat of Rc8+ — after Qd7, only then is Qxh4 safe, and when Black grabs d5, Rc8+ drags the king out to e7. Taking on h4 immediately ran into Nf3+: the knight forked your king and queen, and after Rxf3 the queen on e7 recaptured on h4 straight through the g5 square the knight had just vacated.
drills.title: vs ShoreZee (944) — Caro Kann Defense Classical Variation
drills.coachNote: Before you play Qxh4, check what defends h4 — nothing does, and Black's knight on g5 has a jump to f3 waiting. That's the point: after Qxh4, Nf3+ forks your king on g1 and your own queen on h4 in one move. You're forced to answer with Rxf3, but then the black queen on e7 simply glides down the diagonal and takes your queen on h4 for free — a winning position turns into a lost one because you traded a queen for a knight and a pawn. The h4 pawn wasn't running away; Rdc1 first, doubling rooks and keeping d5 under pressure, sets up the safe version of the same idea.

drills.explanation: Your g2 pawn was pinned — the rook on g6 x-rays it straight to your king — so h3 was really defended only by the rook on f3. Rxf7 removed that defender: Qxh3 crashed in, and Qxg2+ followed with the g6 rook backing the queen up. g4 keeps the queen shut out instead, and the calm Rdd3 and Rd1 regrouping holds; Black wins back only the d5 pawn.
drills.title: vs ShoreZee (944) — Caro Kann Defense Classical Variation
drills.coachNote: Before Rxf7, look at your king — it's on g1 with only the g2 and h3 pawns as shelter, and Black's queen on h4 plus rook on g6 are already aimed at exactly those squares. Grabbing the f7 pawn ignores that: Black plays Qxh3, your cover falls apart, Kf1 doesn't help, and Qxg2+ rips in with check, chasing your king into the open. You were already down badly, but this makes the collapse faster. g4 was the move to look for — it blocks the queen's path to h3 and meets the attack directly instead of spending a move on a pawn across the board.

drills.explanation: The bishop on b5 is giving check, and c6 deals with it perfectly: it blocks and attacks the bishop in one move. After Bxc6+ bxc6 Nxe4 cxd5, the dust settles with you collecting White's d5 pawn — a healthy result. Ke7 also escaped the check but did nothing about your bishop hanging on e4: the knight on c3 took it for free with Nxe4.
drills.title: vs VitaminB5 (926) — Scotch Game
drills.coachNote: Look at your bishop on e4 — nothing defends it, and White's knight on c3 is one hop away from grabbing it. Ke7 does nothing about that, so White just plays Nxe4 and wins the piece for free; afterward the bishop on b5 retreats safely to d3 while you're down material with nothing to show for it. c6 was the move, because it attacks that b5 bishop first — after Bxc6+ bxc6, White still takes on e4 with Nxe4, but now cxd5 scoops up White's d5 pawn as compensation instead of you just losing a piece outright. When one of your own pieces is hanging, deal with it before you spend a move elsewhere.

drills.explanation: Bf6 reroutes the bishop, and the tactics then work for you: when Rf2 hits your bishop on c2, Bxf5! grabs the pawn as it escapes, and after Bxf5 gxf5 you're a pawn up with only Rg3+ to weather. gxf5 won the same pawn but left c2 stranded: Rc1 hunted it down, and Be4 Nxe4 fxe4 left you a weak e4 pawn with White's bishop entrenched on e6.
drills.title: vs VitaminB5 (926) — Scotch Game
drills.coachNote: Before gxf5, two things are hanging: your bishop on c2 has no defender, and your king on g7 only has the g6 pawn as cover. Taking gxf5 opens the g-file while both problems are live — White plays Rc1, your bishop is chased to e4, Nxe4 wins it, and even though fxe4 gets a piece back, your king's left exposed on an open file. Bf6 was the move: it posts a bishop next to your king on f6 first, and only then do the same trades happen on f5 — so when White gets in Rg3+ a couple moves later, your king already has support instead of standing there bare.
